title: Plain naan
subtitle: The leavened flatbread base
excerpt: Soft leavened flatbread cooked in a very hot dry pan until blistered and slightly charred, finished with a brush of melted butter. Plain naan is the neutral bread accompaniment to curry, its slight tang from yoghurt and its soft, chewy crumb make it better for scooping than pita or any unleavened flatbread. The essential bread of the British Indian table.
sourceNotes: Naan (from Persian 'nan', meaning bread) is a leavened flatbread cooked against the inner wall of a clay tandoor. The British curry-house version is cooked in a very hot dry pan or under a grill. Plain naan has been the standard bread offering in British Indian restaurants since the 1960s.

content.text: Plain naan uses the same dough as garlic naan without the flavoured butter. The technique is identical: a soft, yeasted dough rested for an hour, rolled to about 5 mm thick, cooked in a smoking-hot dry pan until blistered, then brushed with plain melted butter while still hot. The butter is applied immediately so it soaks into the hot bread rather than sitting on the surface.

The dough should be soft, softer than most bread doughs. This makes it slightly sticky to handle, which is correct. Flour the surface and the rolling pin rather than adding more flour to the dough, which would tighten it. The finished naan should have some chew but not be dense or heavy; if it is, the yeast did not activate properly or the dough was over-handled after resting.

Mix the {{plain-flour}}, {{fast-action-yeast}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Add the {{plain-yoghurt}}, {{milk}}, and {{vegetable-oil}} and mix to a soft, slightly sticky dough. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth. Place in an oiled bowl, cover, and rest in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled.

Divide the risen dough into 4 pieces. On a floured surface, roll each into an oval about 25 cm long and 4 to 5 mm thick. Heat a dry cast-iron or heavy pan over maximum heat until smoking. Cook each naan for 60 to 90 seconds on the first side until the underside has char spots and the surface bubbles, then flip and cook 60 seconds on the second side. Brush with melted {{unsalted-butter}} immediately. Stack and keep warm under a cloth.

items.fix: Check yeast is in date. The dough must double before rolling, if it hasn't risen after an hour, the room may be too cold or the yeast is dead
items.cause: Yeast not working or dough not risen properly
items.symptom: Dense and doughy rather than soft and chewy

items.fix: The dough should be soft and slightly tacky. Use well-floured hands and surface rather than adding extra flour to the dough
items.cause: Too much flour added during kneading

items.fix: Preheat the dry pan for at least 5 minutes on maximum heat before the first naan
Wrap in foil or a cloth while still warm. Reheat in a hot dry pan or oven, don't microwave, which creates a rubbery texture
items.symptom: Dry and tough the next day

content.text: Plain naan is the neutral bread of the British curry-house table, an accompaniment that does not compete with the curry it accompanies. It is the base from which peshwari and garlic naan variants depart. The soft crumb and slight tang from the yoghurt make it well-suited to scooping sauced dishes. In the ordering hierarchy of a British Indian meal, the naan decision (plain, garlic, or peshwari) is typically made alongside the main course selection, with one naan per person as the standard portion.
